results.title: Facebook Content Moderators Sue Meta in Kenya
results.content: Follow this section to personalize your feed and get instant alerts. Follow this tag to personalize your feed and get instant alerts. Follow this author to personalize your feed and get instant alerts. A total of 43 workers for outsourcing company Sama, who moderated Facebook content, are bringing the lawsuit for what they allege was unlawful dismissal under Kenyan law. Sama, which was hired by Meta to moderate Facebook content from Nairobi in 2019, informed 260 content moderators at the start of the year that were being laid off, according to Foxglove, a technology justice nonprofit that is supporting the lawsuit. Sama said in a statement to TIME it had “not been served yet by any entity on this matter,” adding that “discontinuing the content moderation business was a difficult decision that we made when Sama shifted its focus to computer vision data annotation technology platform and solutions.” Meta declined to comment and representatives for Majorel did not respond to requests for comment in time for publication.

results.title: Africa's content moderators want compensation for job trauma
results.content: That's thanks to content moderators, who constantly review content and delete inappropriate images and videos. Often, these content moderators are employed by subcontractors in countries of the Global South. According to British daily *The Guardian*, lawyers are currently preparing for a lawsuit against Majorel, a company contracted by Meta to engage in content moderation. Content moderators working for Majorel in Ghana's capital Accra told *The Guardian* that they have suffered from depression, anxiety, insomnia, and substance abuse. They believe this is a direct result of their work as content moderators. Around two years ago, former Facebook moderators in Kenya's capital Nairobi filed a lawsuit against Meta and its subcontractors Sama (Samasource) and Majorel. Mercy Mutemi, a lawyer who represented the plaintiffs in the Kenya trial, told Reuters such lawsuits are a "wake-up call" for tech companies to take a stronger stance against human rights violations.

Ghana moderators are suing Meta for the psychological distress they have received from their work. The lawsuit attempted to force Sama and Meta to prioritize the well–being of their employees traumatized by the content they view in their work.
